content.body: in this code is there a particularly correct way to introduce a week "offset"? like to look at the previous week or the next week or 12 weeks from now. can i just append `add({ weeks: n })` to the startOfWeek expression and all the rest of the math will still work?

content.body: bakkot: btw the resolver holding the promise is not the only leak affecting Promise.race, it's just the most egregious. The other one is the resolver functions of the race result being held by a long-lived raced promise. This is common for these to accumulate in cases where you have an "on exit" type promise that gets continuously raced. This case can be generalized as: a resolver function which no longer has any observable effect (because its linked promise was resolved) being held internally in some spec structure (in this case the reaction list of a long lived promise).
content.formatted_body: @bakkot:matrix.org: btw the resolver holding the promise is not the only leak affecting Promise.race, it's just the most egregious. The other one is the resolver functions of the race result being held by a long-lived raced promise. This is common for these to accumulate in cases where you have an "on exit" type promise that gets continuously raced. This case can be generalized as: a resolver function which no longer has any observable effect (because its linked promise was resolved) being held internally in some spec structure (in this case the reaction list of a long lived promise).

content.body: Yeah that's a lot more annoying to fix and also try functions themselves are unlikely to hold much data so it matters a lot less
Like if you think about what it would take to solve it, you end up at a complete refactor of either the promise machinery or GC
The race case doesn't need a full refactor as it can do its own bookkeeping. That's what we do in https://github.com/endojs/endo/blob/master/packages/promise-kit/src/memo-race.js

But also changing the promise machinery to better track promises resolved to other promises (or conditionally resolved in the case of race) is something I've been wanting to explore for a while. I wrote a prototype promise implementation that does some of that a few years ago. Bonus: it would properly detect resolution cycles

You'd need Promises to know when callbacks previously passed to their `.then`s have become no-ops (e.g. because they are resolvers for already-resolved functions) and mark those as available for GC, and given how engines do GC that's a huge lift
content.formatted_body: You'd need Promises to know when callbacks previously passed to their .thens have become no-ops (e.g. because they are resolvers for already-resolved functions) and mark those as available for GC, and given how engines do GC that's a huge lift

content.body: incidentally, Safari has the same `.race` leak I'm fixing in Chrome
content.formatted_body: incidentally, Safari has the same .race leak I'm fixing in Chrome

content.body: I am like 80% sure a sufficient fix is to null out the `JSFunctionWithFields::Field::ResolvingPromise` after these lines https://github.com/WebKit/WebKit/blob/574fccad44f052870183b8d89b7f2f7df2ecfcb9/Source/JavaScriptCore/runtime/JSPromise.cpp#L401-L405
